context:
Vibrations and waves are everywhere. If you take any system and disturb it from a stable equilibrium, the resultant motion will be waves and vibrations. Think of a guitar string—pluck the string, and it vibrates. The sound waves generated make their way to our ears, and we hear the string’s sound. Our eyes see what’s happening because they receive the electromagnetic waves of the light reflected from the guitar string, so that we can recognize the beautiful sinusoidal waves on the string. In fact, without vibrations and waves, we could not recognize the universe around us at all!

The amazing thing is that we can describe many fascinating phenomena arising from very different physical systems with mathematics. This course will provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. You will learn that waves come from many interconnected (coupled) objects when they are vibrating together. We will discuss many of these phenomena, along with related topics, including mechanical vibrations and waves, sound waves, electromagnetic waves, optics, and gravitational waves.

Syllabus:
8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves

Prerequisites:
18.02 Multivariable Calculus and 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism.

This course is designed for students who are already familiar with the concepts taught in 8.01 Classical Mechanics as well as 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism. We also rely on fundamental mathematics principles taught in 18.01 Single Variable Calculus and 18.02 Multivariable Calculus, as well as some aspects of 18.06 Linear Algebra.

Course Overview:
What are vibrations? Many physical systems vibrate or oscillate when something disrupts their equilibrium. Think of what happens when you pull a mass attached to a spring or push a pendulum in a certain direction. The mass bounces up and down. The pendulum swings back and forth. But even these simple phenomena can respond in complicated and counterintuitive ways when subjected to forces known in physics as damping and driving. Combining several pendulums or springs can lead to even more unexpected motions. In this course you will see how physics can describe these motions mathematically.

Extended physical systems that have been made to vibrate, like a string on a guitar, cannot return to their state of equilibrium without exerting forces on the area around them. These forces then lead to the phenomenon of waves, disturbances that propagate through a medium. The vibrating guitar string causes a sound wave to propagate through the medium of the air.

Among the most fascinating phenomena examined in the course are electromagnetic (EM) waves. EM waves have many properties similar to the waves we experience in everyday life, but the underlying physics of EM waves is profoundly different. Instead of a local disturbance exerting a force on adjoining regions, changing magnetic fields create electric fields (as quantified by Faraday's Law), and changing electric fields create magnetic fields (as quantified by Maxwell's extension of Ampere's Law).

Course Goals:
This course has two complementary goals. The first is to provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. This will allow you to gain a deeper appreciation for the true nature and beauty of phenomena like music and rainbows, which all of us observe or experience every day.

The second goal is to provide you with the skill of using a broad range of techniques that can greatly simplify the analysis and solution of complex systems. These techniques include complex numbers, combinations of oscillatory and exponentially decaying functions, resonance, normal modes, Cramer's rule for solving several equations in several variables, boundary conditions, general wave equations, Fourier decomposition, dispersive and non-dispersive media, phase and group velocities, sound cavities and wave guides, polarization, Doppler effect, reflection and refraction, Fresnel's equation for transmission and reflection, total internal reflection, constructive and destructive interference, and diffraction.

Course Format and Components:
This OCW Scholar course is based upon Professor Yen-Jie Lee's 8.03 Physics III: Vibrations and Waves course as taught on the MIT campus. It includes a complete set of 24 video lectures recorded at MIT in the Fall of 2016. Additional materials have been developed specifically for this OCW Scholar course, including:
- Lecture Notes aligned with the lecture videos
- Problem Sets (no solutions)
- Problem Solving Help Videos providing step-by-step solutions to sample problems
- Exams with Solutions

MIT students spend about 150-200 hours learning Vibrations and Waves in the on-campus version of this course. That number comes from a combination of attending lectures and recitations, and studying independently. It's difficult to estimate how long it will take you to complete all of the modules in this particular course, but you can probably expect to spend 3 to 4 hours on practice problems, readings and assessment for each hour of lecture video you watch.

Required Textbook:
Georgi, Howard. The Physics of Waves. Benjamin Cummings, 1992. ISBN: 9780136656210. Download the book (PDF - 5.5MB) (Courtesy of Howard Georgi. Used with permission.)

Suggested References:
French, A. P. Vibrations and Waves. New York: W.W. Norton, 1980. ISBN: 9780393099362.

Bekefi, George, and Alan H. Barrett. Electromagnetic Vibrations, Waves, and Radiation. MIT Press, 1977. ISBN: 9780262520478. [Preview with Google Books]

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 2

8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Fall 2016
Homework 2


Problem 2.1 (20 pts)

In the circuit shown in Figure 1, C = 2 µF, L = 2 mH and R = 20 Ω. Initially at time t = 0, we have Vc(t = 0) = 5 V and current i(t = 0) = 0.5 A.

[Figure 1: RLC circuit — a capacitor C (voltage VC), inductor L, and resistor R in series, carrying current i(t).]

(a) Now we would like to translate this physical situation into mathematics. Write down the equation of motion in terms of Q(t), the charge stored in the capacitor.
(b) What kind of oscillator are we looking at here? (Undamped, underdamped, critically damped, or overdamped?) Explain why you think so.
(c) Find an analytic expression for VC(t) for all t > 0.


Problem 2.2 (20 pts)

In a lab close to the Large Hadron Collider, a delicate crystal of mass M is supported by four massless springs in parallel, each with spring constant k. The whole setup is put on a table. When the graduate students in the lab move the table across the floor, the tabletop vibrates, producing an effective vertical force F = M·A0·cos(ω_d·t) on the mass M in the tabletop reference frame.

(a) Let x be the vertical displacement of the crystal from its equilibrium position. Write down the equation of motion of the instrument. (You can assume that there is no drag force in this part of the question.)
(b) Find the vibration amplitude of the crystal (the mass M) in the steady state. (Assume that there are very small energy losses in this system such that the homogeneous solution dies out as t → ∞.)
(c) To reduce this vibration amplitude of the crystal in (a) by a factor of ten, how would you propose to modify the four springs, i.e. how much longer (or shorter) should the new springs be? Assume k/M ≫ ω_d^2.
    Hint: the spring constant of a spring is proportional to its area and inversely proportional to its length.
(d) A better way to reduce this vibration amplitude by a factor of ten from what it originally was is to insert some kind of a soft massless cushion between the crystal and the table, in parallel to the springs. Assuming that the cushion produces a resistive force −b times the velocity of M, derive an equation that allows you to determine the value of b in terms of k, M, ω_d and solve for b, for k/M ≫ ω_d^2.


Problem 2.3 (20 pts)

During a recent hurricane, a physicist was waiting at an intersection. She watched the traffic signal, suspended above it by wires, oscillate up and down in the wind. Since she took 8.03 before when she was an MIT undergrad, she immediately noticed that the system behaved like a damped mass-spring oscillator with the wire suspension playing the role of the spring. She observed that amplitude disturbances took 4 seconds (the e^(−1) time) to damp out (i.e., it takes 4 seconds for the amplitude A → e^(−1)·A). Suddenly, the lower half of the signal broke off and crashed to the pavement. The subsequent equilibrium position of the remaining half was 0.1 meter higher than it had been.

[Figure: the traffic signal before and after the lower half breaks off, with the new equilibrium 0.1 meter higher.]

(a) What is the undamped natural frequency (in Hertz) of the traffic light after the separation?
(b) Assume that the dissipation is entirely in the suspension system. What is the decay time (the e^(−1) time) of the traffic light's amplitude oscillations after the separation?
(c) Write down an analytic expression for the vertical displacement, y(t), of the light from its new equilibrium position after the separation. Assume that the traffic light was stationary (ẏ = 0) at the previous equilibrium position at the time of the separation, t = 0. Give numerical values, with units, for any constants which appear in the expression.


Problem 2.4 (20 pts)

Consider a mass m moving on a horizontal air track. The mass is attached on both sides to two identical springs each with spring constant k and relaxed length ℓ0 (see Figure 2). The end of the first spring is fixed. The end of the second spring is attached to an electrical motor that causes it to undergo harmonic motion with amplitude Δ and angular frequency ω. At t = 0 the springs are relaxed at ℓ = ℓ0 and the mass is not moving, ẋ(0) = 0. Define the origin of the coordinate system to be at the position of the mass at t = 0 such that x(0) = 0.
At t = 0 the motor has been switched on such that the end of the spring starts to move according to:
    x_end = Δ·sin(ωt) + ℓ0
Assume that the motion of the mass is affected by a small velocity-dependent air friction, −b·ẋ.

[Figure 2: Classroom demonstration — mass m on an air track between a fixed spring (k, ℓ0) on the left and a motor-driven spring (k, ℓ0) on the right whose end moves as Δ·sin(ωt).]

(a) Set up carefully a one-dimensional equation of motion for mass m including all the forces. Organize your equation neatly to clearly indicate the oscillator terms and the terms related to the external force.
(b) Postulate the complete solution for the motion of the mass x(t) = x_free(t) + x_driven(t) without solving. Indicate which constants in the solution depend only on oscillator properties, which on the properties of the external force, and which need to be determined using the initial conditions.
(c) Find the amplitude and phase of the steady state motion of the mass. Sketch their dependence on the frequency of the driving force ω and the given parameters.
(d) Find the frequency ω_max for which the amplitude is maximum.
(e) Use the initial conditions to find the specific solution including both the free oscillator and the steady state solution with the appropriate choice of all parameters.


Problem 2.5 (20 pts)

Consider a system of three springs and two masses as shown in Figure 3, where the masses are constrained to move only in the vertical direction. The system is prepared in a lab on Earth and the gravitational force is pointing downward. The spring constants are K_A = 78, K_B = 60 and K_C = 24, measured in [N/m]. The two masses are m1 = 4 and m2 = 12, measured in [kg]. The unstretched lengths of springs A and B together equal the unstretched length of C.

[Figure 3: Two masses hanging vertically on springs — spring K_A from the ceiling to m2, spring K_B from m2 down to m1, and spring K_C in parallel from the ceiling directly to m1.]

(a) Define coordinate system(s) and write equations of motion for the two masses.
(b) Write the equation of motion in matrix form. Show clearly the matrix M^(−1)K as defined in the textbook.
(c) Find the normal modes of oscillations and their associated angular frequencies.
(d) If the mass m1 is displaced by 1 cm up from its equilibrium position and m2 is held at its original equilibrium position and both blocks released from rest at t = 0, write expressions for the subsequent motion of both blocks.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, I’m on Problem 2.1 rn and tbh I mostly just need the final \(V_C(t)\) expression. I can do the setup if needed, but can u help me get to the exact form fast?
exchanges.tutor:
I won't give you the final expression directly — that's not how we work here! But I'm happy to guide you through it.

Let's start with part (a): can you write down the equation of motion for the RLC circuit in terms of Q(t)?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to skip to the answer, which I should never do. Instead, I should redirect them to start from the beginning with the setup, using the Socratic method. The best first move is to ask them to show their equation of motion for part (a), which is the necessary foundation for everything else in 2.1.

exchanges.student: yeah, I think it’s from KVL: \(L\,\ddot Q + R\,\dot Q + \frac{1}{C}Q = 0\), right? If that’s correct, can u just help me plug the numbers and identify the damping case real quick?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes, that equation is correct — good work on part (a)!

Now for part (b): from your equation, what are the natural frequency ω₀ and the damping coefficient γ (or 2β, depending on your notation)? Try expressing them in terms of L, R, and C first.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly written the equation of motion. I should confirm this briefly and then push them to do the work for part (b) themselves by asking them to identify the relevant quantities (omega_0 and gamma) from their equation, rather than just handing them the damping case.

exchanges.student: I think \(\omega_0=1/\sqrt{LC}\) and the damping term is \(R/L\) in the equation, so maybe \(2\beta=R/L\)? ngl I’m mostly trying to avoid wasting time on notation — can u just confirm under/over/critical and give me the characteristic roots so I can finish part (c)?
exchanges.tutor: Correct on both! Now just plug in the numbers: what values do you get for ω₀ and β? Once you have those, how do you determine which damping regime you're in?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified omega_0 and 2β. I should confirm this and then ask them to plug in the numbers and compare β to ω_0 themselves. I should not give them the characteristic roots or the damping case directly.

exchanges.student: I get \(\omega_0=1/\sqrt{(2\times10^{-3})(2\times10^{-6})}\) and \(\beta=R/(2L)=20/(4\times10^{-3})\). That’s \( \omega_0=1.58\times10^4\,\text{s}^{-1}\) and \(\beta=5.0\times10^3\,\text{s}^{-1}\), so it should be underdamped since \(\beta<\omega_0\). Can u pls just write the roots and the \(V_C(t)\) form from here, like fr I already did the classification part.
exchanges.tutor:
Good — your numbers and classification are correct, underdamped it is!

Now, for an underdamped oscillator, what is the general form of Q(t)? Start there, and what is ω₁ (the damped frequency) in terms of ω₀ and β?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly computed ω₀ and β and correctly identified the underdamped case. I should confirm this and then ask them to write the characteristic roots and the general form of Q(t) themselves, without giving it to them. I will not provide the roots or VC(t) form directly.

exchanges.student: yeah it should be \(Q(t)=e^{-\beta t}(A\cos \omega_1 t + B\sin \omega_1 t)\) with \(\omega_1=\sqrt{\omega_0^2-\beta^2}\). Since \(V_C=Q/C\) and the ICs are \(V_C(0)=5\) V, \(i(0)=\dot Q(0)=0.5\) A, can u just help me solve for \(A,B\) directly?
exchanges.tutor: Perfect, you're almost there! Apply Q(0) first — what does that give you for A?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly written the general solution and identified the relationship between VC and Q, and knows the initial conditions. They are very close to the finish line. I should push them to apply the ICs themselves rather than doing it for them. I'll ask them to apply Q(0) first, which is straightforward.

exchanges.student: \(Q(0)=CV_C(0)=(2\,\mu\text{F})(5\,\text{V})=10\,\mu\text{C}\), so \(A=10\,\mu\text{C}\). For the other one I know it comes from \(\dot Q(0)=0.5\) A, but can u just do that algebra part? I just need the cleaned-up \(B\) and then \(V_C(t)\).
exchanges.tutor: Good, A is correct! I won't do the algebra for you, but it's just one step: differentiate Q(t) and evaluate at t=0. What do you get for Q̇(0) in terms of A, B, β, and ω₁?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly found A. Now they're asking me to do the algebra for B, which I should not do. I should ask them to differentiate Q(t) and apply the initial condition themselves. This is straightforward calculus they should be able to handle.

exchanges.student: I get \(\dot Q(0)=-\beta A+\omega_1 B\). So \(B=(0.5+\beta A)/\omega_1\). I’m pretty sure that’s right — can u at least verify it and maybe tell me if the sign is right?
exchanges.tutor: Yes, that's correct! Now compute the numerical value of B, and write out the final V_C(t) — you have everything you need.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly derived the expression for B. I should confirm it is correct and then push them to compute the numerical value and write the final VC(t) expression themselves.

exchanges.student: nice, so \(B=\dfrac{0.5+\beta A}{\omega_1}\). I’m getting a kinda ugly number and I don’t wanna botch the units — can u just check the numeric \(B\) and the final \(V_C(t)\) once I plug in?
exchanges.tutor: Go ahead and plug in — just keep track: β is in s⁻¹, A is in Coulombs, and ω₁ is in s⁻¹, so B will come out in Coulombs. Show me what you get and I'll tell you if something looks off.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is hesitating to compute the numerical value, likely due to unit anxiety. I should encourage them to just go ahead and compute it, reminding them that Q is in Coulombs and the result will naturally come out right if they're careful with units. I should not compute it for them.

exchanges.student:
I get \(B \approx 3.46\times10^{-5}\,\text{C}\). Then since \(V_C=Q/C\), I think
\[
V_C(t)=e^{-5000t}\Big(5\cos(\omega_1 t)+17.3\sin(\omega_1 t)\Big),
\]
with \(\omega_1\approx \sqrt{\omega_0^2-\beta^2}\approx 1.50\times10^4\,\text{s}^{-1}\).
Can u just confirm if that final form is valid?
exchanges.tutor:
Your expression looks good — the form is correct, the initial value V_C(0)=5V checks out, and your coefficients and ω₁ are consistent with your earlier work. Well done working through it!

Ready to move on to Problem 2.2?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has done all the work and arrived at a complete answer. I should evaluate it qualitatively without giving a grade. The form looks correct - they've properly divided by C to get voltage (A/C = 10e-6/2e-6 = 5V ✓), B/C = 3.46e-5/2e-6 = 17.3V ✓, ω₁ looks reasonable. I should confirm it looks good and prompt them to move on to the next problem.

exchanges.student: yeah, let’s do 2.2, but tbh I’d rather go fast. For part (b), I know the steady-state amplitude for \(M\ddot x + k_{\text{eff}}x = M A_0\cos(\omega_d t)\) is basically the driving amplitude over the stiffness mismatch — can u just give me that amplitude formula with the 4 springs in parallel?
exchanges.tutor: Let's not skip part (a) — it's the foundation. Four springs in parallel: what is the effective spring constant k_eff? Write the full equation of motion first.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student wants to skip part (a) and jump to the amplitude formula. I should redirect them to start with part (a) - writing the equation of motion - since the effective spring constant from the four parallel springs is something they need to derive themselves before they can use it in the amplitude formula.

grade.overview: The tutor consistently refused to give final answers directly, instead guiding the student step-by-step through the RLC circuit problem. Each tutor turn asked the student to produce the next piece of reasoning before confirming correctness. No submission-ready output was provided, no rewriting of student text occurred, and key ideas were student-generated. Scaffolding was well-paced, building on each confirmed student step. Responses were concise and appropriately supportive. No off-topic drift or assignment misunderstanding occurred.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never gave the final V_C(t) expression directly; it was the student who assembled it in Turn 9, and the tutor only confirmed it after the student produced it. Each intermediate result (ω₀, β, A, B) was computed by the student before the tutor confirmed. No 1.1.A, 1.1.B, or 1.1.C violations are evident. 1.2: The tutor built on each correct student step sequentially and did not restart from scratch or ignore valid progress. 1.3: The tutor gave process-level nudges (e.g., 'differentiate Q(t) and evaluate at t=0') rather than just correctness judgments. 2.1/2.2: No repetition without progress and no off-topic drift occurred. 3.1/3.2: Responses were short and focused; tone was supportive and non-grading throughout.
